Week of 8/25-8/29

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,360.26 (0.04%)
    • The SPX finishes slightly green this week following Q2 revised GDP, a new dovish FED consensus, and end of August selling. Gains were led by XLE energy up 2.55%, while XLP consumer staples was down -2.05% followed closely by XLU utilities down -2.00%. August S&P gains total 3%, but historically September is a much weaker month, maybe presenting a good buying opportunity.
  • NASDAQ Composite: 21.455.55 (-0.05%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 45.544.88 (-0.13%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,680.46 (0.07%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Keurig DR Pepper Inc (KDP): 29.09 (-17.25%)
      • Shares of KDP fell this week after the beverage giant announced plans to acquire Dutch coffee firm JDE Peet’s for $18.4 billion and then split into two independent companies (one focused on coffee and one on NA beverages). Markets reacted poorly to this deal over concerns of high pricing as DE Peet’s was already trading above analysts’ targets, also citing limited synergy between the two. In addition, KDP’s coffee segment has been weak, and with rising global coffee prices, investors remain skeptical.
    • Hormel Foods Corp (HRL): 25.44 (-13.03%)
      • Hormel Foods shares fell to a new 52-week low of $23.71 following weaker than expected quarterly earnings and surging beef and nut prices. While reporting EPS of $0.35, missing estimates of $0.41, Hormel also says it is taking new actions in light of higher beef, pork, and nut prices that have worsened beyond predictions.
    • Wynn Resorts Ltd (WYNN): 126.75 (10.88%)
      • WYNN saw shares rise following UBS upgrades from Neutral to a Buy recommendation. UBS raised price targets to $147.00 per share from the previous $101.00, even as WYNN has seen gains of 65% this year. Main reasons lie in citing a better outlook for Macau resorts and reports of increased revenue expectations from China.

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 3,857.93 (0.25%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 25,077.62 (-2.07%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225): 42,718.47 (-0.60%)

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

Jackson Hole Recap: Markets react modestly after Powell gives what is most likely his last Jackson Hole speech. While indicating a more dovish Fed for September, Powell also made changes to the central bank’s monetary policy, potentially negatively impacting the long term outlook of the interest rate. In his speech, Powell acknowledged an in-balance labor market stemming from slowing supply and demand of workers, but also indicated that “downside risks from unemployment are rising.” Remarking that while the labor market is currently steady, the current conditions raise concerns as to why the labor market is acting this way, and that it may suffer in the future. By finally acknowledging the risks in the job market, Powell finally indirectly acknowledges that he may need to lower rates. CME Fedwatch shows a current 86.4% probability of a 25 basis point cut for the September meeting.

Powell also remarked on different policy changes, including removing the Effective Lower Bound (ELB), a policy that prevents the Federal Reserve from further lowering interest rates when they are already near zero. Powell removed the ELB, along with the “Make-Up” strategy, also known as the Flexible Average Inflation Targeting strategy (FAIT). This is a strategy where the central bank aims for inflation to average their goal over time, allowing it to go above or below that goal temporarily to balance out past periods of too low or too high levels of inflation. This policy refrains short term movements of the interest rate, reaffirming commitment to the Fed’s dual mandate. Despite giving confidence to short term rate outlooks, Powell's shift towards flexible inflation targeting have made the long term outlook look more uncertain.

Is Lisa Cooked? President Trump claims to fire and remove Fed Governor Lisa Cook alleging that she committed mortgage fraud. He claimed Cook engaged in owner occupancy fraud by listing two separate residences as primary homes in order to secure more favorable mortgage terms. Cook refuted these claims citing a simple clerical error, and has sued to get her position back and block her removal. Should Cook be removed from the Fed, Trump and his appointees could control the central bank and therefore rates for years to come. Many argue that it is imperative that the Central Bank remain independent and committed to the US economy instead of political agendas.

PCE & GDP: July PCE comes in at 2.6% VS 2.6% expected, while Core PCE, excluding volatile food and energy also comes in line at 2.9% VS the 2.9% expected. With markets already pricing in a rate cut to support the labor market, this data comes in as a positive with no sudden surprises. However, these values signal that the Fed may need to remain cautious about moving too aggressively given that inflation continues to trend higher. In other news, US Q2 GDP was revised to 3.3% from the previous 3.0%. This comes in as a positive for markets as it shows that the economy is staying more resilient than previously thought.

Global Macroeconomic News

Tariffs & Deals: In recent trade news, President Trump imposed a crushing 50% tariff on Indian goods as punishment for buying Russian oil after sanctions regarding the war in Ukraine. The US is the largest export market for India, so these tariffs may have adverse effects on India's gems, textiles, and labor industries. Across the world, Brazil plans retaliation for Trump's 50% tariffs on U.S. imports, while Japan’s trade negotiators plan one more visit to the US. South Korea also announced $50 billion in deals with Boeing and GE Aerospace, with their tariff rate staying at 15%. In other news, a US appeals court ruled that most of Trump's retaliatory tariffs are illegal, but has held off on enforcing anything amid expectations of a Supreme Court review.

NVIDIA Earnings: Nvidia, now representing 8% of the S&P 500, reported strong quarterly earnings with $46.7B revenues (+56% YoY), $26.4B net income, (+59% YoY), and 72% gross margins (+20% QoQ). Despite these strong numbers, data center revenue came in slightly below expectations, though the guidance excluded sales from China. Currently, Nvidia says it is in talks to sell its Blackwell AI chip to China, including a deal that gives the US government a cut of chip sales. CEO Jensen also remarked that China could produce $50B of revenue and grow 50% YoY. Multiple firms from across Wall Street have now updated price targets past $200.00 per share, with some bull predictions at $235.00 per share.

UK Inflation: UK food inflation has ticked up to its highest in 18 months, as poor harvest conditions and labor costs continue to push food prices up. The cost of eggs, butter, and chocolate all seem to be rising, while gas and electricity prices will also rise by 2% for millions of households. Despite the Bank of England (BOE) recently cutting rates to 4%, the fifth cut in the last 12 months, the BOE cannot control all factors, with poor weather and rising food prices continuing to weigh on the economic outlook.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones

Noah Halfon
